dialogue.utterance: Unit 53 responding to 911 for a breathing problem at a residential location on Broad Street, Richmond.
Good evening, ma'am, I'm Kelly, an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 today?
I've been having trouble breathing all day, but it got really bad about an hour ago when I moved from the kitchen to my room.
Can you open your eyes for me, please?
(opens eyes)
I see you opened them spontaneously. That's good. Can you tell me your full name?
My name is Celeste.
Great. Now, if I ask you to lift your finger, can you do that?
(lifts finger)
You're alert and oriented x4. You seem to know your name, where you are, and the date.
Your airway is patent and clear. I don't see anything obstructing it.
Let me check your breathing. Are you finding it hard to take deep breaths?
Respirations are at 23 per minute, rapid and labored. Do you feel more comfortable sitting up?
Your breathing is wheezy, especially during expiration. How much effort are you putting in to breathe right now?
It's very hard. I have to force myself to take a breath.
Let's check your pulse and blood pressure now.
The pulse is strong and rapid at 118 beats per minute.
Blood pressure is 162 over 102. Skin is pink, warm, and dry.
Got it. Are you known for any chronic conditions?
I have COPD and hyperlipidemia.
What medications do you currently take?
She only has an inhaler - the others were lost or not accessible.
Did you take the inhaler before calling 911?
Yes, but it didn't help. I can't catch my breath as before.
At what point did the shortness of breath first appear today?
It began all day, but got much worse after I got from the kitchen to my room.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate your difficulty in breathing right now?
Probably a 9. It feels like gasping for air.
Your skin is dry, normal in color, and has good turgor. Any unusual rashes or bruises?
No, just some old stretch marks.
Your head is normocephalic and your neck is normal with no tenderness or swelling.
Your throat is clear. Any ear, nose, or blood flow issues?
No, nothing out of the ordinary.
You have no chest deformities, and the chest is symmetric and atraumatic.
I'll now listen to your breath sounds. Can you take a deep breath in and out while I check?
Of course.
I hear diffuse expiratory wheezing in both lungs - this is consistent with a COPD exacerbation.
You seem to be using extra effort to breathe - I can see your chest working hard.
Your abdomen is soft and flat with no signs of tenderness.
I don't feel any numbness in the extremities.
Your oxygen saturation is 91 percent. I'll place a nasal cannula for monitoring.
I'll apply the EKG pads briefly - they won't hurt. Thank you for staying still.
The EKG shows sinus tachycardia.
We'll also use an ETCO2 nasal cannula to monitor carbon dioxide levels while on oxygen.
Per protocol, you'll receive nebulizer therapy with Atrovent and Albuterol. I'll start that now.
With your consent, we'll administer the nebulizer now, using 8 LPM oxygen.
This nebulizer has a HEPA filter to keep the air clean during treatment.
Just a dosage of 0.5 mg Atrovent and 5 mg Albuterol via the nebulizer.
This will help alleviate the wheezing as you breathe deeply. Take slow and deep breaths.
After treatment, your pulse is 98, breathing is now 10, and SpO2 at 99. Blood pressure is 113/77.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how bad does it feel now?
Still a bit, but I think 6 now keeps it manageable.
Anything else changed after the nebulizer?
No, just occasional tingling, but that's routine for me.
It looks like you're not in pain. I'll note that in your chart.
Your glucose is 0. We can monitor this, but it doesn't appear in crisis.
A 4-lead ECG has been initiated for continuous monitoring per protocol.
This will monitor ongoing electrical activity. You may feel a slight stick from the pads, but nothing more.
You're improving. Would you like to be transported or handle it at your own pace now?
I feel better, but I'll go to the ER with my daughter in a while. Doesn't need a late-night drive.
We'll provide education on recognizing changes. Would you sign the refusal form with an explanation?
Yes, I'll sign it and call 911 if this flares again. My daughter will accompany me.
That's good. I'll document your refusal, your condition, and medications. Travel safely, and see you if you need anything else.

Celeste, it's important you watch for worsening breathing or other concerning symptoms. If it is not better in the next hour or two, consider calling 911 again. I'll give you some information to help you out.
Here's some education on COPD and how to manage an exacerbation at home. Take deep breaths, and keep that inhaler with you at all times.
If your breathing gets worse or you feel lightheaded or have chest pain, don't wait - call 911 right away. This is part of the education I'm required to give.
I'll now clean off the patches. Also, I'll give you a basic education on what to watch for with your COPD.
Look for worsening shortness of breath. If that happens, call 911 right away.
Unit 53, no issues reported while there. Good work and clear airway, Celeste. Don't hesitate to call us.
Tonight was a good one. More than that - your nebulizer was professional and timely. Thank you for calling.
